summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's record Q4 2007 financial results, focusing on the Mac selling 2.2 million units—a 34% year-over-year increase with half of retail buyers being new Mac users. He argues there's no single explanation but rather a confluence of favorable factors: the iPod halo effect making people simply consider Macs, the switch to Intel enabling Windows compatibility, Microsoft's stagnation with Vista, Apple's retail stores, nerd switchers influencing friends and family, and surging popularity among college students. He contrasts this with the bleak 1990s when the Mac seemed headed for irrelevance, noting that the current sustained growth represents what Mac enthusiasts had hoped for over two decades.
key_points: The iPod halo effect works not through logical reasoning but by simply making people consider buying a Mac for the first time, overcoming the decades-old problem of consumers never even putting Macs on their shopping list.
The 1990s Mac exodus bottomed out around the Mac OS X era, leaving Apple with a small but fiercely loyal base—and now virtually no one is switching from Mac to Windows.
The rise of the web as the dominant platform neutralized Windows' 90-95% market share advantage, since web apps work on every computer regardless of OS.
Intel Macs with Boot Camp/Parallels/VMware made switching less scary by letting users keep Windows as a safety net, paralleling how Classic eased the OS 9 to OS X transition.
Nerds switching to Mac created an outsized influence because they recommend computers to friends and family, amplifying Apple's growth beyond the nerd demographic itself.
money_quotes: Most people buying a new computer never even considered buying a Mac. It's not that they'd never heard of Apple or didn't know the Mac existed, it's that they somehow assumed Macs were for some nebulous group of 'others', when the truth is that many of these people would be delighted with a Mac.
Do you know of anyone — anyone at all — talking today about switching from the Mac to Windows? I don't.
Windows's market share of 90 or 95 percent pales next to the market share of the web, which is effectively 100 percent.
Microsoft hasn't done anything interesting with Windows since XP.
It was a long wait for Vista and it didn't seem worth it.
Nerds are the people who recommend computers to friends and families; it seems inarguable that there are an awful lot of nerds recommending Macs today who weren't five years ago.
What we're seeing now is what Mac enthusiasts have been hoping to see for 20 years: more people deciding to buy a Mac.
key_insight: The Mac's record-breaking growth in 2007 resulted not from any single factor but from a rare convergence—the iPod halo effect, Intel compatibility, web platform dominance, Microsoft stagnation, and nerd evangelism—that finally broke the two-decade pattern of consumers never even considering a Mac.
